summary: Gruber predicts the specifications and features of the next-generation iPhone based on informed sources, framing the expected CPU upgrade as a generational leap akin to the jump from 486 to Pentium processors. He argues the new iPhone will feature a 600 MHz processor (up from 400 MHz), 256 MB RAM, an improved video-capable camera, and a magnetometer. He contends that Flip-style dedicated video cameras are being squeezed from both sides by video-capable still cameras and smartphones. The post concludes with a tiered betting framework for WWDC predictions, expressing high confidence in the hardware upgrade while wagering against any tablet announcement.
key_points: The next iPhone's CPU upgrade will be a generational leap, not just incremental — more than 1.5x faster due to architecture improvements beyond raw clock speed
Current iPhone performance is CPU-constrained more than network-constrained, meaning faster processors will dramatically improve web rendering and app launching
Dedicated pocket video cameras like the Flip are being squeezed from two sides: high-end still cameras gaining video and low-end phones gaining video capability
Apple deliberately hides CPU and RAM specs, treating the processor as 'secret sauce' while only publishing storage capacities
The iPhone's video advantage will come from convenience and software (editing, uploading) rather than raw image quality
money_quotes: A new computer almost always feels faster than the one it replaces. In the old days, though, every few years you'd get a computer with not just a faster processor but a next-generation processor, and the resulting performance increase was dramatic.
Based on information from informed sources, I believe the processor in the next-generation iPhone is going to be that kind of upgrade.
Faster app launching should make it feel more like switching between apps and less like quitting/relaunching them.
Web page rendering on current iPhones is hindered at least as much, if not more, by the CPU than by the speed of the 3G network.
The best camera is the one you have with you, and if you've always got your iPhone, you've always got a camera.
The distinction between 'still' and 'video' cameras is quickly disappearing. Soon they'll just be 'cameras' that do both.

For many casual use cases, being able to upload short clips to the web directly from your iPhone immediately after shooting the footage trumps whatever image quality advantages a camera like a Flip might hold.
key_insight: The next iPhone represents a generational processor leap that will transform perceived performance, while its video capabilities — powered by software and connectivity rather than hardware superiority — will begin displacing dedicated pocket video cameras.
